ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a dedicated Marie biscuit entry; 'biscuit food product' (FOODON_03301710) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 category A000E covers biscuits and crackers broadly; no Marie-biscuit-specific code exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for Marie biscuit; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of Marie biscuits is approximately 4 biscuits (25 g), commonly consumed as a light snack or with tea.

nutrient_source.title: Marie Biscuit — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Marie biscuit (cr_id F11080035). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 70 for Marie biscuit is consistent with published peer-reviewed data for plain sweet wheat-flour biscuits (GI ~69–70, glucose reference). The input record cited GI 70 from an embedded GI database aligned with the Foster-Powell et al. 2002 international GI table. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 18.33) / 100 = 12.83 (based on 25 g serving providing 18.33 g carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 386 mg/100 g combined with high sugar (18.78 g/100 g) and low dietary fibre (1.11 g/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly limit Marie biscuit intake due to borderline-high sodium (386 mg/100 g) and high sugar content (18.78 g/100 g with only 1.11 g fibre), as excess sugar drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and elevates blood pressure; limit to no more than 2–3 biscuits per occasion and choose low-sodium, high-fibre alternatives.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrates (73.33 g/100 g), high sugar (18.78 g/100 g), low dietary fibre (1.11 g/100 g), and high GI (70)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Marie biscuits are unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to high refined carbohydrate content (73.33 g/100 g), high sugar (18.78 g/100 g), negligible fibre (1.11 g/100 g), and a high glycemic index of 70, all of which cause rapid postprandial glucose elevation; if consumed, strictly limit to 1–2 biscuits and pair with a protein source to blunt glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~6.5 g/100 g from palm oil, sodium 386 mg/100 g, and high sugar 18.78 g/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Marie biscuits present a high cardiovascular concern due to estimated saturated fat of approximately 6.5 g/100 g (from palm oil), sodium of 386 mg/100 g, and high sugar (18.78 g/100 g), all of which contribute to dyslipidaemia, elevated blood pressure, and endothelial inflammation; individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption and substitute with wholegrain, low-saturated-fat crackers.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined sugar (18.78 g/100 g), refined carbohydrates (73.33 g/100 g), and palm oil saturated fat (~6.5 g/100 g) promote pro-inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Marie biscuits are unsuitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as refined sugar, refined starch, and palm oil-derived saturated fat collectively upregulate TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokines; replace with wholegrain seed crackers that provide anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids and higher fibre.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Marie biscuit is a light, crisp, round sweet biscuit made primarily from refined wheat flour, sugar, palm oil, and salt, baked to a thin, uniform disc shape. Originating in the United Kingdom and named after the Russian Grand Duchess Maria Alexandrovna, it became widely popular across Europe, Asia, and Singapore as an everyday tea-time snack. The texture is dry and crunchy with a subtle sweetness and a mild buttery-wheat flavour. Per 100 g, Marie biscuits provide 458 kcal, 8.28 g protein, 14.24 g fat (estimated 6.5 g saturated from palm oil), 73.33 g carbohydrate, 18.78 g sugar, 1.11 g dietary fibre, and 386 mg sodium. The glycemic index is high at 70, making these biscuits a rapid-glucose-release food. Clinically, Marie biscuits are unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to their combination of high refined carbohydrates, high sugar, low fibre, borderline sodium, and palm oil saturated fat. They are commonly consumed as a standalone snack, with tea or coffee, or used as a base for no-bake cheesecake crusts and dessert recipes across Singapore and Southeast Asia. Allergen note: contains gluten and wheat.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a Marie biscuit?
Is Marie biscuit suitable for hypertension?
What is the GI of Marie biscuit?
How much sodium in Marie biscuit?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F11080035) with confidence 0.95. GI value of 70 is consistent with peer-reviewed published data for plain sweet wheat-flour biscuits. Saturated fat estimated at ~6.5 g/100 g based on typical palm oil biscuit formulation; this single field is estimated rather than directly sourced, but all other values are exact from HPB.
notes: iron_mg is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source record; this is treated as a confirmed zero per the HPB database entry (confirmed zero per HPB Foodo F11080035). monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this Marie biscuit entry are not reported in the HPB Foodo database as of April 2026. saturated_fat_g of 6.5 g/100 g is an estimate based on typical palm oil biscuit formulation; no direct saturated fat value was reported in the HPB source. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (70 × 18.33 g carbohydrate per 25 g serving) / 100 = 12.83. Marie biscuit is a globally distributed product; no Singaporean-specific cuisine classification applies, and therefore the multilingual naming requirement for Singaporean dishes does not apply to this record.
